steps.title: Identify what the question needs
steps.content: Martinez and colleagues hypothesized that paired leaves should tend to show opposite-side orientation. Supporting data must show that opposite-side counts exceed same-side counts in the species tested. The graph shows results for three grapevine species: graybark grape, Amur grape, and July grape.

steps.title: Eliminate the choices
steps.content:
• Choice A — Although the number of leaf pairs showing same-side orientation is fairly high in the July grape, it is much lower in both the Amur grape and graybark grape.: Focuses on same-side counts and relative comparisons across species without confirming that opposite-side exceeds same-side in all three. Doesn't directly support the hypothesis. ✗
  • Choice B — In the graybark grape, Amur grape, and July grape, all the leaf pairs show opposite-side orientation.: Overstates the data — the graph shows some same-side pairs exist in each species, not zero. ✗
  • Choice C — Although the exact ratio varies by species, the graybark grape, Amur grape, and July grape all show more leaf pairs with opposite-side orientations than with same-side orientations.: Accurately reflects the graph (graybark 270 opposite > 100 same; Amur 150 opposite > 70 same; July 200 opposite > 100 same) and directly supports the hypothesis for all three species. ✓
  • Choice D — The number of leaf pairs showing opposite-side orientation is fairly high in the July grape, but not as high as it is in the graybark grape.: Only compares two species to each other and doesn't address whether opposite-side exceeds same-side — fails to support the hypothesis. ✗
C is correct.
